text: Let me show you laparoscopic DII lymph node dissection in advanced gastric cancer. Thirty-four-year-old female patient had advanced gastric cancer, and we are going to do distal subtotal gastrectomy with DII dissection.
The surgeon identifies and begins dissection of the number four SB lymph nodes, located along the left gastroepiploic vessels, revealing a very small lymph node in this area.

The surgeon clips and divides the left gastroepiploic artery at its origin. The infrapolar lymph nodes, identified as number six, are located along the first branch and proximal part of the right gastroepiploic artery. The right gastroepiploic vein is isolated, clipped, and divided, followed by clipping and dividing the right gastroepiploic artery at its origin.
The surgeon trims the posterior wall of the duodenum, exposing the gastro-duodenal artery. The anticipated duodenal resection line is marked.
The surgeon trims the lesser curvature of the duodenum and divides the duodenum two centimeters from the pyloric ring using a 60mm endo-GIA stapler.

The surgeon exposes the gastroduodenal artery and the right gastric artery at their origins. The right gastric artery is clipped and divided, while number five lymph nodes, identified as suprapolar lymph nodes, are located along the first branch and proximal part of the right gastric artery.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the number twelve A lymph nodes located in the hepatoduodenal ligament along the proper hepatic artery. The left aberrant hepatic artery is visible. Additionally, the number eight A lymph nodes are identified along the common hepatic artery.
The common hepatic artery and portal vein are visible, along with the number eight lymph nodes. The surgeon prepares to move to the left side to dissect the number nine and number seven lymph nodes.

The surgeon identifies number seven lymph nodes along the trunk of the left gastric artery and number nine lymph nodes near the celiac artery. The left gastric artery and its accompanying left gastric vein are visible.

The surgeon identifies the number eleven P lymph node located along the proximal splenic artery from its origin to halfway between its origin and the pancreas tail end. The surgical team proceeds to dissect the distal splenic artery area, taking care to avoid injury to the splenic artery.

The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of number one right paracardial lymph nodes and number three lesser capsule lymph nodes along the branches of the left gastric artery. During the procedure, the vagus nerve is encountered and divided using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon removes the number three lymph nodes and trims the lesser curvature area. The stomach is divided five centimeters proximal to the upper margin of the tumor. The specimen is placed in a bag and extracted through the umbilical wound.

The pericardial lymph nodes are visible after the resection of the stomach. The number four SB area, indicating the origin of the left gastroepiploic artery, is shown. Moving to the right, the celiac axis is visible, along with the number nine, number seven, number eleven P, and number eight A lymph nodes. Further to the right, the stumps of the right gastric artery and right gastroepiploic artery, corresponding to the number six area, are observed.
The surgeon exposes the duodenal stump and proceeds with the dissection of DII lymph nodes, carefully navigating the surrounding anatomical structures.
